text: The patient is positioned in a modified lithotomy position with the head elevated at a 25-degree angle. The surgeon stands between the patient's legs, while the camera operator is on the right and the assistant on the left. Four trocars are inserted into the abdomen, with the laparoscope introduced through a midline port above the umbilicus. The surgeon begins mobilizing the gastric fundus by dissecting the gastroesplenic ligament and dividing the cephalic short gastric vessels, revealing a small fat pad at the stomach's end. The posterior attachments of the upper gastric fundus are divided, opening the rear cavity.

The surgeon identifies the posterior window and dissects a space superior and medial to the free edge of the right crus to facilitate closure of the hiatus. The esophagus is retracted anteriorly and to the left, while the crura are approximated starting at the level of the aortic dissection.

The surgeon passes a forceps behind the esophagus from right to left, grasping the posterior wall of the gastric fundus to form the wrap. A 45 French bougie is inserted to calibrate the fundoplication, followed by placing stitches to secure the wrap, which measures approximately five centimeters in total length.
